topic: A person finds out their surprise party has been cancelled
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the dull thud of a dropped stone. I clicked it open, my jaw tightening instantly. "Party cancelled." The words swam before my eyes, a ridiculous, flimsy excuse for the massive effort everyone had put in. I reread the message three times, the same knot of frustration tightening in my chest with every repetition. I slammed my laptop shut, the sound echoing sharply in the otherwise quiet apartment. I stood up, pacing the length of the living room, my hands clenching and unclenching at my sides. Why? Just why? All those coordinated outfits, the RSVPs, the careful planning—reduced to this flimsy notification. I walked over to the window, staring out at the indifferent streetlights, a simmering resentment building behind my eyes.

[story 2]

"Seriously?" I muttered, the disbelief thick in my voice as I stared at the screen. The text message confirmed what my gut was already screaming: the celebration was off. A slow, hot flush crept up my neck. I felt a sudden, sharp need to check my phone again, to see if this was some elaborate prank, but the confirmation was undeniable. I let out a long, shaky breath, the air tasting stale. I walked over to the kitchen counter and gripped the edge until my knuckles turned white. I picked up a mug, my fingers trembling slightly, and stared into the lukewarm coffee. It tasted like ash. The sheer waste of anticipation made my shoulders slump, heavy with a profound sense of letdown.

[story 3]

I reread the cancellation notice, my eyes scanning the text as if searching for a hidden loophole, a typo that would somehow invalidate the entire thing. A low growl started deep in my throat. This wasn't just disappointing; it felt like a deliberate slight. I shoved the phone into my pocket with unnecessary force. I started cleaning the already spotless surfaces in the room, scrubbing the dust off the bookshelf with aggressive, jerky movements. Each swipe of the cloth felt like an attempt to physically erase the absurdity of the situation. My breathing became shallow and quick. I wanted to shout, to demand an explanation, but the words caught in my throat, leaving only a tight, pressurized feeling behind.
